Name: dynamic_layer_selection_inference
Title: Dynamic Layer Selection for Latency-Aware Inference Using Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the geometric properties of activation distributions can be utilized to dynamically adjust the number of layers utilized during inference, leading to improved latency and accuracy without requiring training.
Related Work: While existing works like SQR and OptimML focus on quantization and power management, our proposal introduces a novel method for active layer selection based on real-time activation metrics, filling a gap in the literature regarding dynamic model scaling without retraining.
Abstract: In this proposal, we explore a novel approach to optimizing machine learning inference through dynamic layer selection based on activation geometry. We hypothesize that real-time metrics, such as the spectral condition number of activations, can inform which layers are necessary for a given input, allowing for a training-free mechanism to adapt the depth of the model. Our method employs a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that enables the model to skip unnecessary computations while maintaining accuracy within strict latency budgets. This mechanism not only enhances efficiency but also aligns with PAC-Bayes principles, ensuring bounded overhead while adapting to varying input complexities. We will evaluate our approach across multiple tasks and architectures, measuring throughput and accuracy to establish a Pareto frontier for latency-aware inference. By providing a rigorous, empirically validated framework, this work aims to contribute significantly to the field of latency-sensitive applications in machine learning.
Experiments: Implement a baseline model and the proposed dynamic layer selection method on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
Measure inference latency and accuracy across various input complexities to establish the Pareto frontier.
Conduct ablation studies to evaluate the impact of different layer-skipping strategies on performance metrics.
Collect cross-layer covariance statistics to validate the spectral condition number hypothesis against actual performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method may not generalize well across all architectures or tasks, requiring careful selection of benchmarks.
Dynamic adjustments could lead to unpredictable performance if activation distributions are not adequately characterized.
Real-time computations may introduce additional overhead that offsets the benefits of layer skipping.
